question:
Problem A-2
Let \( a, b, p_{1}, p_{2}, \ldots, p_{n} \) be real numbers with \( a \neq b \). Define \( f(x)=\left(p_{1}-x\right)\left(p_{2}-x\right)\left(p_{3}-x\right) \cdots\left(p_{n}-x\right) \). Show that
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{lllllll}
p_{1} & a & a & a & \cdots & a & a \\
b & p_{2} & a & a & \cdots & a & a \\
b & b & p_{3} & a & \cdots & a & a \\
b & b & b & p_{4} & \cdots & a & a \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots & \vdots \\
b & b & b & b & \cdots & p_{n-1} & a \\
b & b & b & b & \cdots & b & p_{n}
\end{array}\right)=\frac{b f(a)-a f(b)}{b-a} .
\]
solution:
A-2.
Let \( M_{t} \) be the matrix obtained by subtracting \( t \) from each entry of the given matrix and let \( G(t) \) be the determinant of \( M_{t} \). By subtracting the entries of any row from the corresponding entries of each other row, one sees that \( G(t) \) is linear in \( t \). Then one notes that \( G(a)=f(a) \) and \( G(b)=f(b) \) using the fact that they are determinants of triangular matrices. Then linear interpolation shows that the desired determinant \( G(0) \) is
\[
[b G(a)-a G(b)] /(b-a)=[b f(a)-a f(b)] /(b-a)
\]

descriptive_long.question:
Problem A-2
Let \( constanta, constantb, rootone, roottwo, \ldots, rootlast \) be real numbers with \( constanta \neq constantb \). Define \( polyfun(variable)=\left(rootone-variable\right)\left(roottwo-variable\right)\left(rootthree-variable\right) \cdots\left(rootlast-variable\right) \). Show that
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{lllllll}
rootone & constanta & constanta & constanta & \cdots & constanta & constanta \\
constantb & roottwo & constanta & constanta & \cdots & constanta & constanta \\
constantb & constantb & rootthree & constanta & \cdots & constanta & constanta \\
constantb & constantb & constantb & rootfour & \cdots & constanta & constanta \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots & \vdots \\
constantb & constantb & constantb & constantb & \cdots & rootpenult & constanta \\
constantb & constantb & constantb & constantb & \cdots & constantb & rootlast
\end{array}\right)=\frac{constantb\, polyfun(constanta)-constanta\, polyfun(constantb)}{constantb-constanta} .
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
A-2.
Let \( shiftedmat \) be the matrix obtained by subtracting \( auxvar \) from each entry of the given matrix and let \( detfunc(auxvar) \) be the determinant of \( shiftedmat \). By subtracting the entries of any row from the corresponding entries of each other row, one sees that \( detfunc(auxvar) \) is linear in \( auxvar \). Then one notes that \( detfunc(constanta)=polyfun(constanta) \) and \( detfunc(constantb)=polyfun(constantb) \) using the fact that they are determinants of triangular matrices. Then linear interpolation shows that the desired determinant \( detfunc(0) \) is
\[
[constantb\, detfunc(constanta)-constanta\, detfunc(constantb)] /(constantb-constanta)=[constantb\, polyfun(constanta)-constanta\, polyfun(constantb)] /(constantb-constanta)
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Problem A-2
Let \( candlestick, arrowshield, sailcloth, treelizard, \ldots, moonflower \) be real numbers with \( candlestick \neq arrowshield \). Define \( lanternfly(marbleseed)=\left(sailcloth-marbleseed\right)\left(treelizard-marbleseed\right)\left(spiderling-marbleseed\right) \cdots\left(moonflower-marbleseed\right) \). Show that
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{lllllll}
sailcloth & candlestick & candlestick & candlestick & \cdots & candlestick & candlestick \\
arrowshield & treelizard & candlestick & candlestick & \cdots & candlestick & candlestick \\
arrowshield & arrowshield & spiderling & candlestick & \cdots & candlestick & candlestick \\
arrowshield & arrowshield & arrowshield & canyonwind & \cdots & candlestick & candlestick \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots & \vdots \\
arrowshield & arrowshield & arrowshield & arrowshield & \cdots & p_{n-1} & candlestick \\
arrowshield & arrowshield & arrowshield & arrowshield & \cdots & arrowshield & moonflower
\end{array}\right)=\frac{arrowshield \, lanternfly(candlestick)-candlestick \, lanternfly(arrowshield)}{arrowshield-candlestick} .
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
A-2.
Let \( rainstorm \) be the matrix obtained by subtracting \( granitebox \) from each entry of the given matrix and let \( corridor(granitebox) \) be the determinant of \( rainstorm \). By subtracting the entries of any row from the corresponding entries of each other row, one sees that \( corridor(granitebox) \) is linear in \( granitebox \). Then one notes that \( corridor(candlestick)=lanternfly(candlestick) \) and \( corridor(arrowshield)=lanternfly(arrowshield) \) using the fact that they are determinants of triangular matrices. Then linear interpolation shows that the desired determinant \( corridor(0) \) is
\[
[arrowshield \, corridor(candlestick)-candlestick \, corridor(arrowshield)] /(arrowshield-candlestick)=[arrowshield \, lanternfly(candlestick)-candlestick \, lanternfly(arrowshield)] /(arrowshield-candlestick)
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Problem A-2
Let \( limitless, confined, expansive, boundless, \ldots, eternity \) be real numbers with \( limitless \neq confined \). Define \( unchanging(fixedvalue)=\left(expansive-fixedvalue\right)\left(boundless-fixedvalue\right)\left(measureless-fixedvalue\right) \cdots\left(eternity-fixedvalue\right) \). Show that
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{lllllll}
expansive & limitless & limitless & limitless & \cdots & limitless & limitless \\
confined & boundless & limitless & limitless & \cdots & limitless & limitless \\
confined & confined & measureless & limitless & \cdots & limitless & limitless \\
confined & confined & confined & unending & \cdots & limitless & limitless \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots & \vdots \\
confined & confined & confined & confined & \cdots & ceaseless & limitless \\
confined & confined & confined & confined & \cdots & confined & eternity
\end{array}\right)=\frac{confined\, unchanging(limitless)-limitless\, unchanging(confined)}{confined-limitless} .
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
A-2.
Let \( singlescalar \) be the matrix obtained by subtracting \( timeless \) from each entry of the given matrix and let \( triviality(timeless) \) be the determinant of \( singlescalar \). By subtracting the entries of any row from the corresponding entries of each other row, one sees that \( triviality(timeless) \) is linear in \( timeless \). Then one notes that \( triviality(limitless)=unchanging(limitless) \) and \( triviality(confined)=unchanging(confined) \) using the fact that they are determinants of triangular matrices. Then linear interpolation shows that the desired determinant \( triviality(0) \) is
\[
[confined\, triviality(limitless)-limitless\, triviality(confined)] /(confined-limitless)=[confined\, unchanging(limitless)-limitless\, unchanging(confined)] /(confined-limitless)
\]

garbled_string.question:
Problem A-2
Let \( wexafnup, cidromal, ufmaclep, zirdonex, \ldots, hanjoklu \) be real numbers with \( wexafnup \neq cidromal \). Define \( hdusipwq(vaqsitlum)=\left(ufmaclep-vaqsitlum\right)\left(zirdonex-vaqsitlum\right)\left(qbafliro-vaqsitlum\right) \cdots\left(hanjoklu-vaqsitlum\right) \). Show that
\[
\operatorname{det}\left(\begin{array}{lllllll}
ufmaclep & wexafnup & wexafnup & wexafnup & \cdots & wexafnup & wexafnup \\
cidromal & zirdonex & wexafnup & wexafnup & \cdots & wexafnup & wexafnup \\
cidromal & cidromal & qbafliro & wexafnup & \cdots & wexafnup & wexafnup \\
cidromal & cidromal & cidromal & mgrailto & \cdots & wexafnup & wexafnup \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \vdots & & \vdots & \vdots \\
cidromal & cidromal & cidromal & cidromal & \cdots & yodgreps & wexafnup \\
cidromal & cidromal & cidromal & cidromal & \cdots & cidromal & hanjoklu
\end{array}\right)=\frac{cidromal \, hdusipwq(wexafnup)-wexafnup \, hdusipwq(cidromal)}{cidromal-wexafnup} .
\]
garbled_string.solution:
A-2.
Let \( qmaorbel \) be the matrix obtained by subtracting \( zyoqkner \) from each entry of the given matrix and let \( iklabsyv(zyoqkner) \) be the determinant of \( qmaorbel \). By subtracting the entries of any row from the corresponding entries of each other row, one sees that \( iklabsyv(zyoqkner) \) is linear in \( zyoqkner \). Then one notes that \( iklabsyv(wexafnup)=hdusipwq(wexafnup) \) and \( iklabsyv(cidromal)=hdusipwq(cidromal) \) using the fact that they are determinants of triangular matrices. Then linear interpolation shows that the desired determinant \( iklabsyv(0) \) is
\[
[cidromal \, iklabsyv(wexafnup)-wexafnup \, iklabsyv(cidromal)] /(cidromal-wexafnup)=[cidromal \, hdusipwq(wexafnup)-wexafnup \, hdusipwq(cidromal)] /(cidromal-wexafnup)
\]

kernel_variant.question:
Let $n\ge 2$ be an integer.  Fix real numbers  

\[
p_{1},p_{2},\ldots ,p_{n}\qquad (\text{the diagonal data})
\]

and two $n$-vectors  

\[
u=(u_{1},u_{2},\ldots ,u_{n})^{\mathsf T},\qquad 
v=(v_{1},v_{2},\ldots ,v_{n})^{\mathsf T}\qquad 
(\text{the row/column parameters})
\]

such that  
\[
p_{i}\neq u_{i}+v_{i}\qquad\text{for every }i=1,\ldots ,n. \tag{$\dagger$}
\]

Define the $n\times n$ matrix $M=(m_{ij})$ by  

\[
m_{ii}=p_{i},\qquad m_{ij}=u_{i}+v_{j}\;\;(i\neq j). \tag{$\star$}
\]

Put  

\[
d_{i}=p_{i}-u_{i}-v_{i}\quad(\text{so }d_{i}\neq 0\text{ by }(\dagger)), 
\qquad D=\operatorname{diag}(d_{1},\ldots ,d_{n}).
\]

(a) Prove that  

\[
\det M=\Bigl(\prod_{i=1}^{n} d_{i}\Bigr)\,\bigl[(1+A)(1+B)-C\,E\bigr], \tag{1}
\]

where  

\[
A=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{u_{i}}{d_{i}},\qquad 
B=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{v_{i}}{d_{i}},\qquad 
C=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{1}{d_{i}},\qquad 
E=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{u_{i}v_{i}}{d_{i}}. \tag{2}
\]

(b) Show that the determinant collapses to the rank-$1$ form  

\[
\det M=\Bigl(\prod_{i=1}^{n} d_{i}\Bigr)\,(1-E) \tag{3}
\]

if and only if the four rational sums $(A,B,C,E)$ satisfy the single algebraic relation  

\[
A+B+AB=(C-1)E. \tag{4}
\]

Give two non-trivial, explicitly parametrised families of data $(p,u,v)$ that meet condition (4).

Provide complete justification for every assertion.
kernel_variant.solution:
Throughout write  

\[
d_{i}=p_{i}-u_{i}-v_{i},\qquad D=\operatorname{diag}(d_{1},\ldots ,d_{n}).
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (a).  Writing $M$ as a rank-two perturbation of $D$
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Introduce the vectors and covectors  

\[
x:=u,\qquad y:=\mathbf 1,\qquad 
\alpha^{\mathsf T}:=\mathbf 1^{\mathsf T},\qquad 
\beta^{\mathsf T}:=v^{\mathsf T},
\]

where $\mathbf 1$ denotes the column vector $(1,1,\ldots ,1)^{\mathsf T}$.  
For $i\neq j$ we have  

\[
(D+x\alpha^{\mathsf T}+y\beta^{\mathsf T})_{ij}=0+u_{i}\!\cdot\!1+1\!\cdot\! v_{j}=u_{i}+v_{j},
\]

while for $i=j$  

\[
(D+x\alpha^{\mathsf T}+y\beta^{\mathsf T})_{ii}=d_{i}+u_{i}\!\cdot\! 1+1\!\cdot\! v_{i}=p_{i}.
\]

Hence  
\[
M=D+x\alpha^{\mathsf T}+y\beta^{\mathsf T}. \tag{5}
\]
Thus $M$ is a rank-$2$ update of the invertible diagonal matrix $D$.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Woodbury's determinant lemma
--------------------------------------------------------------------
For any invertible matrix $N$ and any $n\times k$ matrices $U,V$ one has  

\[
\det(N+UV^{\mathsf T})=\det N\cdot\det(I_{k}+V^{\mathsf T}N^{-1}U). \tag{6}
\]

Here $k=2$ and  

\[
U=[\,x\; y\,],\qquad V=[\,\alpha\; \beta\,].
\]

Consequently  

\[
\det M=\det D\;\det\!\bigl(I_{2}+V^{\mathsf T}D^{-1}U\bigr). \tag{7}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Evaluating the $2\times 2$ auxiliary determinant
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Because $D$ is diagonal, $D^{-1}=\operatorname{diag}(1/d_{1},\ldots ,1/d_{n})$.  
A direct multiplication gives  

\[
V^{\mathsf T}D^{-1}U=
\begin{pmatrix}
\alpha^{\mathsf T}D^{-1}x & \alpha^{\mathsf T}D^{-1}y\\[2pt]
\beta^{\mathsf T}D^{-1}x  & \beta^{\mathsf T}D^{-1}y
\end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
A & C\\[2pt]
E & B
\end{pmatrix}, \tag{8}
\]
with $A,B,C,E$ as in (2). Hence  

\[
\det\!\bigl(I_{2}+V^{\mathsf T}D^{-1}U\bigr)
=\det
\begin{pmatrix}
1+A & C\\[2pt]
E & 1+B
\end{pmatrix}
=(1+A)(1+B)-C\,E. \tag{9}
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Putting the pieces together
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Since $\det D=\prod_{i=1}^{n}d_{i}$, equations (7) and (9) give precisely formula (1), completing part (a). \hfill $\square$



--------------------------------------------------------------------
Part (b).  When does the determinant reduce to rank $1$?
--------------------------------------------------------------------

Necessity.  Imposing the rank-$1$ collapse (3) on the general formula (1) requires  

\[
(1+A)(1+B)-C\,E=1-E. \tag{10}
\]

Expanding the left side yields  

\[
1+A+B+AB-CE=1-E. \tag{11}
\]

Cancelling the leading $1$'s shows that (10) is equivalent to  

\[
A+B+AB=(C-1)E, \tag{12}
\]
i.e. to condition (4).  Thus (4) is necessary.

Sufficiency.  Conversely, if (4) holds then the identity (11) is valid, so (10) follows and with it (3).  Hence (4) is also sufficient. \hfill $\square$



--------------------------------------------------------------------
Two non-trivial parametrised families satisfying (4)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
We rewrite (4) once more as  
\[
(A+1)(B+1)=1+CE. \tag{13}
\]

Both examples below meet this equation for all admissible data in the family.

Family I - sign-anti-symmetric parameters  
Fix positive numbers $p_{1},\ldots ,p_{n}$ with $\sum_{i=1}^{n}1/p_{i}=1$.  
Let $w=(w_{1},\ldots ,w_{n})^{\mathsf T}$ be any vector satisfying  
$\sum_{i=1}^{n}w_{i}/p_{i}=0$, and set  

\[
u=w,\qquad v=-w.
\]

Then $d_{i}=p_{i}$, so  
\[
C=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{1}{p_{i}}=1,\qquad 
A=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{w_{i}}{p_{i}}=0,\qquad 
B=-A=0,\qquad 
E=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{w_{i}(-w_{i})}{p_{i}}=-\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{w_{i}^{2}}{p_{i}}.
\]
Hence $A+B+AB=0$ and $(C-1)E=0$, verifying (4) for every choice of $w$.  
The determinant therefore collapses to (3).

Family II - constant row/column parameters  
Fix arbitrary diagonal data $p_{1},\ldots ,p_{n}$ and choose constants $a,b\in\mathbb R$ satisfying  

\[
ab+a+b=0\qquad\Longleftrightarrow\qquad (1+a)(1+b)=1. \tag{14}
\]

(Thus neither constant equals $-1$.)  Define  

\[
u=(a,a,\ldots ,a)^{\mathsf T},\qquad v=(b,b,\ldots ,b)^{\mathsf T}.
\]

Then $d_{i}=p_{i}-(a+b)\neq 0$ by assumption, and  

\[
C=\sum_{i=1}^{n}\frac{1}{d_{i}},\qquad 
A=aC,\qquad 
B=bC,\qquad 
E=abC.
\]

Substituting into (4) gives  

\[
A+B+AB=aC+bC+abC^{2},\qquad (C-1)E=(C-1)abC.
\]

Equality holds because $a+b+ab=0$ by (14).  Thus every admissible choice of $(p_{i})$ with $p_{i}\neq a+b$ satisfies (4).  
For instance $a=1,\;b=-\tfrac12$ or $a=-\tfrac13,\;b=\tfrac12$ produce valid data.

Both families are genuinely multi-parameter and illustrate the delicate balancing embodied in equation (4). \hfill $\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Rank-Two Perturbation:  
   The original problem involved a single auxiliary parameter and could be handled
   by elementary row operations leading to a linear polynomial.  The enhanced
   variant treats a diagonal matrix modified simultaneously by two independent
   rank-one updates, forcing the use of the full Woodbury (rank-k) determinant
   formula.  This is a substantial theoretical leap.

2. More Variables and Parameters:  
   Instead of two scalars (a,b) the new matrix depends on 3n independent
   parameters (pᵢ, uᵢ, vᵢ), creating a vastly richer configuration space and far
   more complicated algebraic expressions.

3. Interaction of Concepts:  
   The solution blends several advanced topics – low-rank updates, block
   determinants, and careful bookkeeping of symmetric sums – none of which appear
   in the original statement.

4. Non-trivial Specialisation:  
   Part (b) asks for an additional simplification under linear constraints on the
   data, compelling the contestant to manipulate the general formula further and
   detect hidden cancellations; this layer of subtlety is entirely absent from
   the source problem.

5. Absence of Pattern Matching:  
   Elementary pattern recognition (e.g. “subtract a from each entry”) is no
   longer adequate.  One must recognise the rank-two structure, recall an
   advanced determinant identity, and carry out a sequence of non-routine
   computations.

Consequently the enhanced variant is markedly more demanding both technically
and conceptually than the original kernel problem.

original_kernel_variant.question:

\[

A+B+AB=aC+bC+abC^{2},\qquad (C-1)E=(C-1)abC.
\]

Equality holds because $a+b+ab=0$ by (14).  Thus every admissible choice of $(p_{i})$ with $p_{i}\neq a+b$ satisfies (4).  
For instance $a=1,\;b=-\tfrac12$ or $a=-\tfrac13,\;b=\tfrac12$ produce valid data.

Both families are genuinely multi-parameter and illustrate the delicate balancing embodied in equation (4). \hfill $\square$

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Rank-Two Perturbation:  
   The original problem involved a single auxiliary parameter and could be handled
   by elementary row operations leading to a linear polynomial.  The enhanced
   variant treats a diagonal matrix modified simultaneously by two independent
   rank-one updates, forcing the use of the full Woodbury (rank-k) determinant
   formula.  This is a substantial theoretical leap.

2. More Variables and Parameters:  
   Instead of two scalars (a,b) the new matrix depends on 3n independent
   parameters (pᵢ, uᵢ, vᵢ), creating a vastly richer configuration space and far
   more complicated algebraic expressions.

3. Interaction of Concepts:  
   The solution blends several advanced topics – low-rank updates, block
   determinants, and careful bookkeeping of symmetric sums – none of which appear
   in the original statement.

4. Non-trivial Specialisation:  
   Part (b) asks for an additional simplification under linear constraints on the
   data, compelling the contestant to manipulate the general formula further and
   detect hidden cancellations; this layer of subtlety is entirely absent from
   the source problem.

5. Absence of Pattern Matching:  
   Elementary pattern recognition (e.g. “subtract a from each entry”) is no
   longer adequate.  One must recognise the rank-two structure, recall an
   advanced determinant identity, and carry out a sequence of non-routine
   computations.

Consequently the enhanced variant is markedly more demanding both technically
and conceptually than the original kernel problem.
